coredata.dc:title: An improved practice to manufacture Al–Ti–B master alloys by reacting halide salts with molten aluminium
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Birol, Yücel
coredata.dc:description: The well-established “halide salt” route was employed in the present work to produce Al–Ti–B grain refiner alloys with consistent, good properties. The holding step in the production cycle was revised, however, to avoid oxidation of the molten alloy which is believed to be responsible for the relatively low Ti recoveries and thus for the inadequate and inconsistent grain refining efficiency. Stirring during holding was found to degrade the grain refining properties when molten potassium aluminium fluride salt was left on the molten alloy to avoid excessive oxidation. Likewise, holding temperatures higher than 800°C and holding times longer than 30min both had an undesirable effect on the grain refining performance. The experimental Al–5Ti–1B grain refiner alloy produced according to the present method provided consistent and better overall grain refining performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metals
Casting
Al–Ti–B master alloy
Grain refinement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys processed with a 30min holding step at (a) 750°C, (b) 800°C, (c) 850°C, and (d) 900° C.
The grain refinement performances of the grain refiner alloys processed with a 30min holding step at (a) 750°C, (b) 800°C, (c) 850°C, and (d) 900° C.
The microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys processed (a) without any holding and by holding the molten alloy at 800°C for (b) 5min, (c) 15min, (d) 30min, and (e) 120min.
The grain refinement performances of the grain refiner alloys processed (a) without any holding and by holding the molten alloy at 800°C for (b) 5min, (c) 15min, (d) 30min, and (e) 120min.
The microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys processed (a) without any stirring, (b) with mechanical and (c) magnetic stirring during holding.
The grain refinement performances of the grain refiner alloys processed (a) without any stirring, (b) with mechanical and (c) magnetic stirring during holding.
Effect of holding temperature during processing on the Ti recovery in the final grain refiner alloy
Effect of holding time during processing on the Ti recovery in the final grain refiner alloy
Effect of stirring during holding on the Ti recovery in the final grain refiner alloy

article.ce:title: An improved practice to manufacture Al–Ti–B master alloys by reacting halide salts with molten aluminium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Institute, Marmara Research Center, TUBİTAK, Gebze, Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The well-established “halide salt” route was employed in the present work to produce Al–Ti–B grain refiner alloys with consistent, good properties. The holding step in the production cycle was revised, however, to avoid oxidation of the molten alloy which is believed to be responsible for the relatively low Ti recoveries and thus for the inadequate and inconsistent grain refining efficiency. Stirring during holding was found to degrade the grain refining properties when molten potassium aluminium fluride salt was left on the molten alloy to avoid excessive oxidation. Likewise, holding temperatures higher than 800°C and holding times longer than 30min both had an undesirable effect on the grain refining performance. The experimental Al–5Ti–1B grain refiner alloy produced according to the present method provided consistent and better overall grain refining performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of Al–Ti–B master alloys for grain refinement has been an established practice in aluminium foundries eversince boron additions were shown to remarkably improve grain refinement of aluminium by titanium at hypoperitectic concentrations [1]. The microstructure of commercial alloys, with 2–10wt.% Ti and 0.1–2wt.% B, typically consists of TiAl3 and TiB2 particles in an aluminium matrix with extremely small amounts of Ti and B in solution. The aluminium matrix dissolves and the particles which subsequently act as heterogeneous nucleation sites are released into the melt upon addition. The mechanism of grain refinement by Al–Ti–B master alloys, although still a subject of debate, involves segregation of solute Ti to the TiB2/melt interface and the formation of an interfacial layer which takes part in the nucleation process [2]. Detailed discussion on theories of grain refinement can be found in [3].

Among various methods which have been offered for the production of Al–Ti–B grain refiner alloys [3–18], that involving the reaction of halide salts (KBF4–K2TiF6) with molten aluminium is the most popular. The halide salts are fed to a stirred body of molten aluminium where they are reduced to Ti and B, producing a melt with a dispersion of TiAl3 and TiB2 particles. The molten alloy thus obtained may be cast either as a billet for extruding into a rod or directly as a rod using a continuous rod caster. However, the results obtained in regard to grain refinement with Al–Ti–B alloys thus produced, differ appreciably, since their microstructure and performance as a grain refiner are highly sensitive to the processing parameters used in production [19]. Attempts were made to produce improved grain refiners with consistent performance [20,21] and to better understand the nature of the reaction between aluminium, KBF4 and K2TiF6 [22]. It has recently been reported that holding the master alloy melt at approximately 750°C for several hours after the salt reaction, produces master alloys having very good grain refining properties [23,24].

The present work was undertaken to identify an improved practice for the production of Al–Ti–B grain refiner master alloys with consistent and good performance. The method used in the present work also relies on the reaction of halide salts with molten aluminium, yet is different from the widely employed process as it allows the by-product of the salt reaction, molten potassium aluminium fluoride, to remain on the surface of the molten Al–Ti–B alloy until before casting. The intention, in doing so, was to avoid oxidation of the molten alloy during holding which is believed to be responsible for the relatively low Ti recoveries in the final alloy and thus for the inadequate and inconsistent grain refining efficiency. The effect of various process parameters such as holding temperature, holding time, and stirring during holding on the microstructure and grain refining behavior of the master alloys were studied in detail.

The “halide salt” route for the production of Al–Ti–B master alloys was considered to consist of four distinct and consecutive steps: melting the aluminium ingot; adding the fluoride salts into the aluminium melt to establish a reaction between the two; holding the molten alloy thus obtained under pre-determined conditions and finally casting the molten alloy after decanting the molten potassium aluminium fluoride salt and mixing it thoroughly. A series of experimental Al–5Ti–1B alloys were produced in the laboratory in order to optimize the parameters employed during holding to ensure an adequate grain refining efficiency when molten potassium aluminium fluoride is retained on the surface of the molten Al–5Ti–1B alloy until before casting in order to avoid excessive oxidation.

Production campaigns were carried out on a 1.5kg batch scale. A 1070 alloy ingot (99.7% Al, main impurities iron and silicon) was placed into a graphite crucible and was brought to 800°C using a medium-frequency induction furnace to facilitate fast heating. The K2TiF6 and KBF4 salts of commercial purity, weighted and mixed in proportions so as to achieve a Ti to B ratio of 5 in the final alloy, were added to the melt at once. The salt mixture was gently immersed into the melt without introducing any stirring. The molten alloy thus obtained was held at different temperatures ranging from 750 to 900°C for 5–120min once the temperature increase due to the exothermic reaction between the halide salts and the aluminium melt came to a stop. The overall reaction leading to the formation of TiAl3 and TiB2 compounds, during the production of Al–Ti–B master alloys was reported to be [22]:
(1) 12Al+3K2TiF6 +2KBF4 →2TiAl3 +TiB2 +5KAlF4 +K3AlF6

Holding was performed both in an induction furnace to maintain electromagnetic stirring during holding and also after transferring the crucible to an electric resistance furnace. No stirring was available in the latter, in contrast to the induction stirring customary in industrial practice. Yet, additional mechanical stirring was employed during holding in a separate experiment. Another experiment was conducted without any holding at all. Finally, the potassium aluminium fluoride salts (KAlF4–K3AlF6), by-products of the reaction, were decanted and the melt was stirred thoroughly with graphite rods before it was cast into a permanent mold. The Ti pick-up in each of the experimental alloys was measured with an Phillips X-ray Fluorescence Spectrometer calibrated for Ti.

The experimental master alloys thus produced were assessed for their grain refining performances. Aluminium ingot with a purity of 99.7% Al, weighting 1000g, was melted in a medium frequency induction furnace and its temperature was brought to 710°C. A reference sample was taken from the melt in order to identify the grain size before inoculation. 4g of the experimental alloy was then added to the melt. This is the exact amount of an Al–5Ti–1B master alloy needed to bring the Ti concentration of 1kg aluminium melt to 0.02%. The melt was stirred with a graphite rod for 30s right after inoculation. Samples were taken from the melt 2, 5, 15, 30 and 60min after the addition and were solidified in small copper molds with a diameter of 25mm and a height of 50mm. Measures were taken to keep the temperature of the melt within 710±5°C during the entire process. The samples thus produced were sectioned 20mm from the bottom surface. Standard metallographic procedures were employed to prepare these sections for grain size measurements. They were etched with Poulton's reagent and were then examined under a light microscope.

Ti recoveries in a series of experimental Al–5Ti–1B grain refiner master alloys, produced by holding the molten alloy in an electric resistance furnace between 750 and 900°C for 30min once the temperature increase in the melt upon salt addition was over, were all higher than 98% (Table 1 ). These Ti recoveries are better than those obtained with another group, produced in exactly the same manner except for the holding step which was employed after the molten potassium aluminium fluoride was first removed [25]. The oxidation of the molten alloy during holding without the protection provided by the molten salt cover is thus held responsible for the lower Ti recoveries in the latter set. This account also explains the small decrease in Ti recovery with increasing holding temperatures in the present case.

The microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys processed with a 30min holding step between 750 and 850°C were found to be very similar (Fig. 1 ). TiAl3 particles exhibited predominantly blocky morphologies (i.e. roughly equiaxed chunky particles) in this temperature range and were smaller than 20μm. Several petal-like aluminide particles were noted when processing involved holding at 900°C. These observations are consistent with those of Arnberg et al. [26] who have shown that the aluminides revealed a compact, blocky morphology when they formed at low temperatures while “petal-like” particles dominated at higher temperatures. The TiB2 particles, on the other hand, were invariably smaller than 1μm at all holding temperatures and their morphology was independent of the holding temperature.

The grain refining response of the Al–5Ti–1B alloys produced by holding the melt at 750 and 800°C were almost identical (Fig. 2 a and b). Complete conversion of coarse columnar grain structure to fine equiaxed grains occurred within 2min of inoculation with those alloys which were held below 800°C for 30min after the salt reaction. In addition, the fine equiaxed grain structure was retained until 60min after inoculation. The very fine grain size shortly after inoculation and the absence of fading for contact times of 60min is attributed to the blocky morphology and to the wide size range of TiAl3 particles, the largest of which was approximately 20μm. There was a very slight degradation in the grain refinement performance when the molten alloy was held at 850°C after the salt reaction. Loss in the grain refining effect has become very prominent when the holding temperature was increased further to 900°C (Fig. 2d), possibly due to the increasing number of petal-like aluminide particles. A master alloy with mostly petal-like aluminide particles was reported to be much less effective as a grain refiner than its counterpart with blocky particles [26]. It was thus concluded that the grain refining efficiency was affected little, if any, at holding temperatures between 750 and 850°C but was largely impaired at higher temperatures.

Additional experiments were performed to identify the effect of holding time on the performance of the grain refiner alloy. The holding temperature in these experiments was fixed at 800°C as this particular temperature was found to give the highest Ti recovery. The Ti recovery in the grain refiner alloy was approximately 78% when the molten alloy was cast shortly after the salt reaction, i.e. without any holding (Table 2 ). Holding the molten alloy in an electric resistance furnace at 800°C for only as much as 5min has already produced an increase in the Ti recovery. The Ti recovery which was 84% after 5min of holding during production, improved further with increasing holding times until 30min suggesting that the salt reaction was probably not complete in the initial stages of holding. The Ti recovery in the alloy produced by a 2h holding step, however, dropped to 93%. The lower Ti content of the grain refiner alloy at long holding times is believed to be associated with the oxidation of the molten alloy in spite of the protection provided by the molten salt cover.

The microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys produced with different holding times after the salt reaction are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The TiAl3 particles were, once again, of the blocky variety. They were very small and very few, however, when the molten alloy was cast without any holding, suggesting that it takes a while for the reaction between the halide salts and the aluminium melt to reach completion (Fig. 3a). This is evidenced also by the rather low initial Ti recoveries in the case of short holding times. TiAl3 particles grew larger when the molten alloy was held longer at 800°C after the salt reaction (Fig. 3b). The population of both TiAl3 and TiB2 particles increased with increasing holding times until 15min. There was hardly any change upon further holding and the microstructural features of the grain refiner alloys held at 800°C for longer than 15min were very similar (Fig. 3c–e).

The duration of the holding step was found to have an impact on the grain refining efficiency of the master alloys as one would expect in view of their Ti contents and their microstructural features (Fig. 4 ). The first set of samples in Fig. 4 were inoculated with a master alloy produced without any holding (alloy 5). It is fair to conclude from Fig. 4a that an Al–5Ti–1B alloy so produced, is not a sufficiently good grain refiner. The grain refining performance improved with increasing holding time and was acceptable after 15min (Fig. 4c). Holding the molten alloy for 15min or longer yields a master alloy which does a great job of refining the cast grain structure. The grain refining response of these alloys with similar Ti contents and similar microstructural features (alloys 2,7, and 8) were almost identical (Fig. 4c–e). The improved grain refining properties of these alloys is clearly linked with their relatively higher Ti contents and higher TiAl3 and TiB2 populations. Holding for more than 30min does not seem to offer any improvement in the grain refining response and is thus not necessary. This finding of the present work contrasts that of Guzowski et al. [24], who claimed that the grain refining response improved when the master alloys were held for an appreciable time (up to 2h) after the chemical reaction between the salts and the aluminium melt.

The grain refining response of these alloys, however, were different (Fig. 6 ). Inoculation with the Al–5Ti–1B alloy produced without any stirring during holding (alloy 2) gave fine, equiaxed grains across the section for contact times up to 60min. The Al–5Ti–1B alloy (alloy 9), stirred mechanically in the molten state during holding, on the other hand, has produced a coarse grain structure with columnar grains near the edges (Fig. 6b). Alloy 10, which was held at 800°C for 30min after the salt reaction in an electric induction furnace to introduce magnetic stirring, was a better grain refiner than alloy 9. Inoculation with alloy 10 has produced equiaxed grains across the section of the test sample, which were relatively coarser than those obtained in the case of alloy 2. Stirring of the molten alloy during holding, particularly when introduced mechanically, clearly had a detrimental effect on the grain refining efficiency of the master alloy. The loss of grain refining effect is believed to be associated with the mixing of the potassium aluminium fluoride salt with the molten alloy as a consequence of the stirring action employed during holding. This is probably why mechanical stirring hurts the grain refining properties a great deal more than magnetic stirring does when the salt is left on the molten alloy. The salt entrapped in the molten alloy might have taken part in the wetting of the boride particles, impairing their potency as nucleation sites and leading to their agglomeration at the same time. Emulsification of the alloy and the salt and agglomeration of TiB2 particles was found to occur due to the wetting of the boride particles by the potassium cryolite salt leading to poor grain refining efficiency in a previous work [27].

Stirring during holding was found to degrade the grain refining properties of the Al–5Ti–1B master alloy when molten potassium aluminium fluride salt was left on the molten alloy to avoid excessive oxidation. Likewise, holding temperatures higher than 800°C and holding times longer than 30min both had an undesirable effect on the grain refining performance. In view of the above, a sound process to produce an Al–5Ti–1B master alloy which ensures an adequate grain refining performance is claimed to comprise melting aluminium metal either in an induction or electric resistance furnace; addition of pre-mixed salts to molten aluminium in the temperature range 750–800°C at once to facilitate a rapid salt reaction, gently mixing the salts with the melt without introducing any stirring; holding the melt between 750 and 800°C for 15–30min; decanting the molten salt and throughly mixing the melt before casting into billets for further mechanical working into rods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: It is a pleasure to thank C. Kubilay, O. Cakir, and F. Alageyik of Marmara Research Center for their help in the experimental part of this work.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: P. Davies, J.L.F. Kellie, D.P. Patron, J.V. Wood, US Patent 6,228,185 (2001).
D.K Young, W.C. Setzer, F.P. Koch, R.A. Rapp, M.J. Pryor, N. Jarrett, US Patent 5,415,708 (1995).
D.K. Young, W.C. Setzer, F.P. Koch, R.A. Rapp, M.J. Pryor, N. Jarrett, US Patent 5,484,493 (1996).
M. Alliot, J.-C. Beguin, M. Moutach, J.-C. Percheron, US Patent 3,961,995 (1976).
L. Backerud, R Kiusalaas, H. Klang, M. Vader, J. Noordegraaf, E.H.K. Nagelvoort, US Patent 5,104,616 (1992).
Y. Miyasaka, Y. Masuda, US Patent 3,857,705 (1974).
